title: Singleness
subtitle: What the Bible Says About Singleness
summary: Scripture treats singleness not as a waiting room for marriage but as a legitimate, valued state — one that Paul actually preferred and Jesus himself modeled.
body:
In a culture that treated marriage as the default, Paul's words are striking: I wish that all were as I myself am — single [verse:1cor.7.7]. He goes further, arguing that the unmarried person is free to be concerned about the things of the Lord without the divided attention that marriage brings [verse:1cor.7.32-34].

Jesus lived his entire earthly life unmarried. His wholeness, purpose, and relational depth were not diminished by singleness. He also taught that some are called to remain unmarried for the sake of the kingdom [verse:matt.19.12].

Isaiah contains a remarkable promise to those who feared that childlessness — a consequence of singleness in the ancient world — would erase their legacy. God promises the faithful eunuch an everlasting name better than sons and daughters [verse:isa.56.4-5]. Legacy in God's economy is not biological.

Psalm 68 declares that God sets the lonely in families [verse:ps.68.6]. For the single believer, the church community is meant to be that family — not a substitute for marriage but a genuine expression of belonging.

Singleness is not a deficiency to be solved or a season to be endured. Paul calls it a gift — a different gift than marriage, but a gift nonetheless [verse:1cor.7.7]. Both states have their unique freedoms and their unique challenges.

verses.annotation: Paul calls singleness a gift — not a consolation prize but a genuine charism.
The single person's freedom to focus undivided attention on the Lord's work.
Jesus acknowledges those who remain single for the kingdom's sake.
God promises the faithful eunuch a legacy better than biological children.
God sets the lonely in families — the church as genuine family for the single.
Delight in the Lord and he will give the desires of your heart — desire shaped by relationship with God.

scholars.quote: Fee argues that Paul's preference for singleness in 1 Corinthians 7 was not anti-marriage but pro-mission — his concern was freedom for undivided service, especially given the urgency he felt about the gospel's advance.
scholars.source: The First Epistle to the Corinthians (Fee, NICNT, 1987)

scholars.quote: Oswalt reads Isaiah 56:4-5 as a radical inclusion — in a culture where legacy required offspring, God promises the faithful eunuch something better than biological descendants: an eternal name in his house.
scholars.source: The Book of Isaiah, Chapters 40-66 (Oswalt, NICOT, 1998)

scholars.quote: Keener notes that Jesus' unmarried status was unusual for a Jewish rabbi and would have been culturally conspicuous — his singleness was a deliberate model, not an oversight.
scholars.source: A Commentary on the Gospel of Matthew (Keener, 1999)
